knowledge.statement: The Single Responsibility Principle states that a class or module should have only one reason to change. Keeping components focused on a single concern makes them easier to test, understand, and modify without unintended side effects.

The Open/Closed Principle states that software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification. New behaviour is added by writing new code, not by changing existing code that already works.

The Liskov Substitution Principle states that objects of a subclass must be substitutable for objects of their parent class without altering program correctness. Violations usually mean the inheritance hierarchy is wrong.

The Interface Segregation Principle states that clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they do not use. Large interfaces should be split into smaller, more specific ones so that implementing classes only need to know about the methods that are relevant to them.

The Dependency Inversion Principle states that high-level modules should not depend on low-level modules; both should depend on abstractions. This decouples components and makes the system easier to test and replace.

DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) means every piece of knowledge should have a single, unambiguous, authoritative representation in a system. Code duplication is not just a style issue — it creates multiple places that must be updated when the logic changes, introducing bugs.
KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid) states that most systems work best if they are kept simple rather than made complicated. Simplicity should be a key goal and unnecessary complexity should be avoided.

YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It) means you should not add functionality until it is necessary. Building features speculatively wastes time and adds complexity that must be maintained even if the feature is never used.

Hexagonal Architecture (Ports and Adapters) places the application core at the centre, surrounded by ports (interfaces) and adapters (implementations). This isolates business logic from frameworks, databases, and external services, making it testable without infrastructure.

Clean Architecture organises code into concentric layers: entities, use cases, interface adapters, and frameworks. The Dependency Rule states that source code dependencies must point inward, so inner layers know nothing about outer layers.

Domain-Driven Design (DDD) centres software design around the business domain and domain logic. It introduces concepts like entities, value objects, aggregates, repositories, and domain events to model the problem space explicitly.

A Bounded Context in DDD is an explicit boundary within which a domain model is defined and applicable. Different bounded contexts may use the same term with different meanings, and each context should have its own model.

An Aggregate in DDD is a cluster of domain objects treated as a single unit for data changes. The Aggregate Root is the only object that external objects can hold references to, ensuring invariants are enforced within the boundary.

Event Sourcing stores the state of an application as a sequence of events rather than the current state. The current state is derived by replaying events, giving a complete audit log and the ability to reconstruct past states.

CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) separates read and write operations into distinct models. The write side handles commands that change state; the read side handles queries optimised for display, often using a denormalised read model.

Event-driven architecture uses events to communicate between loosely coupled services. Producers emit events without knowing who consumes them; consumers subscribe to event types they care about, enabling independent scaling and deployment.

Microservices architecture structures an application as a collection of small, independently deployable services each focused on a specific business capability. Services communicate over APIs or message queues and can be deployed, scaled, and replaced independently.

A monolith is a single deployable unit where all components run in the same process. Monoliths are simpler to develop, test, and deploy initially; microservices are preferable when team or scaling boundaries require independent deployment.

The Strangler Fig pattern migrates a monolith to microservices incrementally by routing traffic for specific features to new services while the old system handles the rest. This avoids a high-risk big-bang rewrite.

The Circuit Breaker pattern stops calling a failing service after a threshold of failures and returns a fallback response. After a timeout, it allows a limited number of test requests to check if the service has recovered.

Bulkhead isolation partitions resources (thread pools, connection pools) by consumer so that a failure in one part of the system does not exhaust resources for another. Named after the watertight compartments in a ship hull.

The Saga pattern manages distributed transactions across multiple services using a sequence of local transactions. Compensating transactions undo completed steps if a later step fails, maintaining consistency without distributed locks.

The API Gateway pattern provides a single entry point for clients to access multiple backend services. It handles cross-cutting concerns like authentication, rate limiting, and request routing, reducing coupling between clients and services.

The Sidecar pattern deploys a helper container alongside a main application container in the same pod or host. The sidecar handles cross-cutting concerns like logging, proxying, or configuration without modifying the main application.

The CAP theorem states that a distributed data store can guarantee only two of three properties: Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance. Since network partitions are unavoidable, systems must choose between consistency and availability during a partition.

BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent) is an alternative to ACID for distributed systems. It accepts temporary inconsistency in exchange for high availability and partition tolerance.

Eventual consistency guarantees that, given enough time without new updates, all replicas of a piece of data will converge to the same value. It trades immediate consistency for availability and performance.

Idempotency means that applying the same operation multiple times produces the same result as applying it once. Designing APIs and message handlers to be idempotent is critical for safe retries in distributed systems.

REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for distributed hypermedia systems. Key constraints include stateless communication, uniform interface, client-server separation, cacheability, and layered system.

RESTful API versioning strategies include URI path versioning (/v1/resource), header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.api+json;version=1), and query parameter versioning. URI versioning is most visible; header versioning is most RESTful.

GraphQL allows clients to request exactly the data they need, reducing over-fetching and under-fetching. A single endpoint handles all queries and mutations, with the schema serving as a contract between client and server.

gRPC uses Protocol Buffers for serialisation and HTTP/2 for transport, providing strongly typed contracts, bidirectional streaming, and significantly lower latency than REST for internal service communication.

The Twelve-Factor App methodology defines twelve best practices for building scalable, maintainable SaaS applications: codebase, dependencies, config, backing services, build/release/run, processes, port binding, concurrency, disposability, dev/prod parity, logs, and admin processes.

Separation of concerns means that different aspects of a program (UI, business logic, data access) should be handled by distinct, minimally overlapping modules. Violations make code harder to change because unrelated things are entangled.

Law of Demeter (Principle of Least Knowledge) states that a module should not know about the internal workings of objects it manipulates. A method should only call methods on its own class, parameters, objects it created, or direct component objects.

Composition over inheritance prefers building complex behaviour by combining simple objects rather than inheriting from base classes. Inheritance creates tight coupling between parent and child; composition is more flexible and easier to test.

The Outbox pattern reliably publishes events to a message broker by first writing both the state change and the event to the same database transaction, then a separate process publishes pending events. This prevents the dual-write problem.

The Backend for Frontend (BFF) pattern creates a separate backend service for each frontend client type (mobile, web, TV). Each BFF is optimised for its client's data needs, avoiding a single generic API that poorly serves all clients.

Anti-corruption Layer (ACL) translates between two subsystems with different models or semantics, protecting the internal model from being corrupted by external concepts. Used when integrating legacy systems or third-party services.

Conway's Law observes that organisations design systems that mirror their own communication structures. Teams that cannot communicate effectively tend to produce architectures with poor integration between the components they own.

The Shared Kernel pattern in DDD designates a small, shared subset of the domain model maintained by multiple teams. Changes to the shared kernel require coordination, so the kernel should be kept as small as possible.

Coupling measures how much a module depends on other modules. Low coupling is desirable because it allows modules to be changed, tested, and deployed independently. Cohesion measures how strongly related a module's responsibilities are — high cohesion is desirable.

The Pipe and Filter architectural pattern processes data through a sequence of independent processing steps (filters) connected by channels (pipes). Each filter reads input, transforms it, and writes output, enabling easy reuse and reordering of processing steps.

The Layered (N-Tier) architecture organises code into horizontal layers where each layer only communicates with the layer directly below it. Common layers are presentation, application, domain, and infrastructure.

The Service Mesh pattern handles service-to-service communication concerns (retries, timeouts, circuit breaking, mTLS, observability) in a sidecar proxy rather than in application code. Istio and Linkerd are common implementations.
Reactive systems are responsive, resilient, elastic, and message-driven. They use asynchronous message-passing to achieve loose coupling and handle failures gracefully through isolation.

The Choreography pattern for microservices has each service react to events and produce new events without a central orchestrator. Choreography is more decoupled than orchestration but harder to trace and reason about globally.

The Orchestration pattern for microservices uses a central coordinator service to direct other services through a workflow. Orchestration is easier to understand and trace but introduces coupling to the orchestrator.

Feature toggles (feature flags) decouple deployment from release, allowing new code to be deployed but not activated for users. This enables trunk-based development, A/B testing, canary releases, and instant rollback without redeployment.

Domain events are first-class objects that represent something that happened in the domain. They decouple the cause (a state change) from its effects (side effects in other aggregates or services), and form the backbone of event-driven architectures.

The Repository pattern abstracts data access behind an interface that resembles an in-memory collection of domain objects. Business logic works against the repository interface, remaining ignorant of whether data comes from a database, API, or cache.

The Specification pattern encapsulates business rules as composable objects. Specifications can be combined with AND, OR, and NOT operations to build complex queries and validation rules without embedding logic in entities or repositories.

Immutable infrastructure means servers are never modified after deployment; new deployments replace old instances with freshly provisioned ones. This eliminates configuration drift and makes rollback as simple as redeploying the previous artifact.

The Two-Phase Commit protocol (2PC) ensures all participants in a distributed transaction either all commit or all roll back. It provides strong consistency but is slow, blocking, and unavailable if the coordinator fails.

Back-pressure is a mechanism in reactive streams where a consumer signals to a producer to slow down when it cannot keep up. Without back-pressure, fast producers overwhelm slow consumers, causing unbounded memory growth or data loss.

The Value Object in DDD is an object defined by its attributes, not its identity. Two value objects with the same attributes are equal. Value objects should be immutable — changing one creates a new one.

Shared-nothing architecture ensures each node in a cluster is independent and self-sufficient, sharing no disk, memory, or network. This enables near-linear horizontal scalability because there are no shared resources to contend for.

The Dispatcher pattern routes incoming requests to the appropriate handler based on message type or content. It decouples senders from receivers and is the foundation of command buses and event buses.

Service discovery allows services to find each other dynamically without hardcoded addresses. Client-side discovery has the client query a registry; server-side discovery uses a load balancer to query the registry on the client's behalf.

The Retry pattern automatically re-executes a failed operation, often with exponential backoff. Retries should be used only for transient failures; retrying non-idempotent operations on permanent failures causes data corruption.
